name: Data Exfiltration Pattern
title: Large after-hours upload from {host} to {cloud_domain}
description: Host {host} transferred approximately {mb} MB to unsanctioned file-sharing site {cloud_domain} at 02:00 local time, shortly after {user} accessed an unusually large number of files on SRV-FILE01.
objective: Establish whether the large after-hours upload from {host} represents data theft, identify what data was staged, and determine if this is an insider action or a compromised account.
checklist: Quantify the upload: destination, volume, and time of day
Verify whether {cloud_domain} is a sanctioned corporate service
Check file-share access by {user} in the hours before the upload (collection phase)
Look for staging behavior: archive creation (zip/rar) on {host}
Assess account context: is {user}'s activity pattern consistent with their role and hours?
Determine insider vs. compromised account, and disposition
hints: Exfiltration is a chain: collect → stage → transfer. Work backwards from the upload — search index=windows host=SRV-FILE01 user={user} for the collection phase.
Check sysmon on {host} for an archiving process (7z, rar) writing a large file shortly before the upload — staging is the strongest confirmation.

Insider vs. compromise: check for anomalous logon sources or malware indicators on {host}. Interactive local activity + no malware artifacts points toward insider; either way the response differs (HR/legal vs. IR).
expectedEvidence: Proxy events showing the large upload to an unsanctioned domain
Bulk file-share access preceding the upload
Archive/staging activity on the source host
Assessment of whether the account shows compromise indicators
explanation: This shows the complete exfiltration kill chain: bulk collection from a file share (T1039), local staging into an archive, and after-hours upload to personal cloud storage (T1567.002). Volume alone is a weak signal — backups and legitimate cloud sync move gigabytes — which is why the checklist builds the chain: unsanctioned destination + preceding bulk collection + staging + off-hours timing together are compelling. Disposition is 'needs escalation' because data theft, whether by an insider or via a compromised account, mandates involvement of HR/legal/IR and evidence preservation to a forensic standard — a SOC analyst confirms and escalates, but must not confront the user or unilaterally delete anything.
reportTemplate: 1. Preserve proxy logs and the staging archive on {host} forensically. 2. Suspend {user}'s access pending review — coordinate with HR/legal first. 3. Identify exactly which files were accessed on SRV-FILE01. 4. Block {cloud_domain} for unsanctioned use. 5. Escalate to insider-risk/IR.

events.message: An attempt was made to access an object. Object Name: D:\Shares\Engineering\designs_{port}.pdf Access: ReadData Account: {user}
Process Create: Image: C:\Program Files\7-Zip\7z.exe CommandLine: 7z a C:\Users\{user}\Documents\backup_photos.7z D:\staging\* ParentImage: C:\Windows\explorer.exe
